summary: Ihsan, 'spiritual excellence' or 'doing what is beautiful', is defined in the hadith of Gabriel as worshipping God 'as though you see Him, and if you do not see Him, He nonetheless sees you'. In that report it names the interior, perfecting dimension of religion alongside outward submission (islam) and belief (iman), and Sufi authors identify it with the goal of their path.
description:
The term ihsan derives from a root meaning to do what is good or beautiful. Its classic theological reference point is the hadith of Gabriel, a widely transmitted report in which the angel Gabriel questions the Prophet in turn about islam, iman and ihsan. There islam is answered by the outward acts of submission (the testimony of faith, prayer, almsgiving, fasting and pilgrimage), iman by inner belief in God, His angels, books, messengers, the Last Day and the divine decree, and ihsan by the Prophet's words, 'that you worship God as though you see Him, and if you do not see Him, He nonetheless sees you'. On the reading of this hadith common in the tradition, the three terms mark ascending dimensions of religion: practice, faith, and the excellence or beauty that perfects them, an inward disposition of God-consciousness informing outward act and inner belief.

Sufi authors take ihsan, so defined, as the very object of their path, glossing it as the perfecting interior dimension of religion cultivated through self-purification, the remembrance of God (dhikr) and the discipline of the heart. Contemporary scholars such as Sachiko Murata and William Chittick have used the islam-iman-ihsan schema of the hadith of Gabriel as an organizing framework for the whole of the Islamic religious life, with ihsan naming its spiritual or contemplative dimension. This entry reports the definition and the tradition's use of the term descriptively.
editorial_notes: Scope: ihsan as a theological and spiritual category defined by the hadith of Gabriel and taken up by Sufism as the goal of the path. Reported descriptively, not in a devotional register, per neutrality principle #4. The islam-iman-ihsan triad is presented as the reading of the hadith common in the tradition, attributed rather than asserted as the single correct interpretation (principles #1 and #2). Category set to 'spirituality'. Relations: related_to iman (both are defined together in the same hadith; iman is an existing entry), part_of sufism (Sufi authors take ihsan as the goal of the path), related_to dhikr (a sibling entry in this batch, the practice through which ihsan is cultivated). Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality principle #8: (a) the primary text, the hadith of Gabriel as transmitted in the canonical collections of al-Bukhari and Muslim; (b) a contemporary Muslim scholarly work built around this very hadith and its islam/iman/ihsan structure (Murata and Chittick, The Vision of Islam, web-verified); (c) a modern academic history of Sufism (Karamustafa) for the tradition's adoption of ihsan as the goal of the path. Confidence medium: works verified to exist and be on-topic, relevant passages not read line-by-line. Peer-review fix (v0.1.0 promotion pass, 2026-07-19): the Karamustafa source url previously pointed to a Journal of Qur'anic Studies review of the book (on euppublishing.com) rather than to the book itself; it has been replaced with the book's own JSTOR record. Citation text was already correct and is unchanged.

relations.note: Ihsan is defined alongside islam and iman in the hadith of Gabriel, as the perfecting interior dimension beyond outward practice and inner belief.
Sufi authors take ihsan, 'to worship God as though you see Him', as the very goal of the spiritual path.
The abiding God-consciousness that ihsan names is cultivated in Sufism above all through dhikr, the remembrance of God.

historical_period.note: Rooted in the prophetic hadith of Gabriel (early 7th century CE); taken up as the goal of the path in the formative Sufism of the 3rd/9th century and after.

sources.citation: The Hadith of Gabriel (Hadith Jibril), transmitted in Sahih al-Bukhari (Kitab al-Iman) and Sahih Muslim (Kitab al-Iman). Classical primary source: the Prophet defines islam, iman and ihsan, glossing ihsan as 'that you worship God as though you see Him, and if you do not see Him, He nonetheless sees you'. Relevant passages consulted via standard canonical references, not collated across recensions here.
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; cited for reference.

sources.citation: Murata, Sachiko, and William C. Chittick. The Vision of Islam. New York: Paragon House, 1994. A contemporary Muslim scholarly work structured around the hadith of Gabriel, treating islam, iman and ihsan as the three dimensions of the religion, with ihsan as its spiritual dimension. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.

Karamustafa, Ahmet T. Sufism: The Formative Period. The New Edinburgh Islamic Surveys. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press, 2007. On the interior perfecting of religion (ihsan) as an orienting aim of the Sufi tradition. Relevant passages not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; text not reproduced.
